text:
This was a couple of months ago now, but it’s relevant to what is going on around social media, Pauline Hanson, misinformation, fake news, AI-produced content & overseas accounts that generate the majority of this rubbish.

‘Research from ABC News Verify & The Guardian confirms that many large, seemingly grassroots pro-One Nation Facebook groups operate as foreign-run "meme factories". These networks are managed by offshore administrators in Southeast Asia (such as Vietnam & the Philippines) & are designed to monetize Australian online engagement through ad revenue rather than legitimate political support’

The abc news have identified at least 20 foreign-run pages with many linked to admins based in Sri Lanka, Vietnam & The Philippines. These pages rely on high volumes of AI-generated content - deepfakes, bogus articles, fabricated stories about political figures to drive outrage and generate interest.

The pages exist to game algorithms & direct traffic to external sites, which are profit driven.

The same techniques are used via foreign  Facebook accounts using an AI Pauline Hanson to manipulate Australian users. Posts involving Hanson are generally AI images with a small number of genuine pictures that depict Hanson in a positive & formative light in events that have never happened. Philanthropic roles in the community depict Hanson as a caring individual giving money away & helping people. Other “news stories” depict Hanson taking on other politicians - usually Anthony Albanese - painting the PM in a bad light.

All the so-called news & events that are generated have never happened - but these events & imagery are being liked &  shared throughout social media thousands of times by users who believe what they see without question & can’t see past the fake stories & imagery - these people aren’t prepared to research factual information that would negate this outside of the platform. Simple online searches would result in confirmation that these stories & events are not present in real life or in any news sources outside of the online platform source.

While Pauline Hanson has primarily been depicted in a positive light - Albanese has been depicted in negative events, argumentative and duplicitous. None of these events are true.

These fake news and events posts are used to engage users, encouraging emotive responses - and are more likely to induce anger and resentment. 

According to The Guardian Australia, pro One Nation facebook groups appear to be run by foreign meme factories that monetise content. 

‘Guardian Australia examined 14 of the largest pro-One Nation public groups with at least 8,000 members, and found most were created this year.

While some groups appear to be longstanding and set up by genuine supporters, the majority are full of content overwhelmingly fed by what digital media researcher Timothy Graham said appeared to be “a foreign-run, predominantly Indonesian, for-hire engagement farm operation”

Much of the content across these groups is designed to be what Graham called outrage or “poll bait” – asking yes or no questions, such as “Was Pauline Hanson right to scold this journo?” or “Should Sharia law be banned in the Australia?”
Other posts are reactive, with some of the accounts Guardian Australia tracked posting multiple times about the party’s “Fire the Liar” campaign. In at least one case, the text and image promoting the party’s fundraising drive was copied from a verified One Nation page’

So, pro Hanson content on social media driven by foreign players for positive pro-Hanson political content that is entirely fictitious….

I wonder who is driving this and how much of it is being paid for by Australian taxpayers?

It would appear there is much to be gained for some but not others via online fake media stories, memes and AI.

Really elegant work on fixing variational RL with diffusion models using self-distillation! 👏👏

*Problem*: Likelihood gradients ∇ log p(a) are needed for RL but are hard to compute for diffusion models. A useful surrogate is ∇ ELBO(a), where the ELBO is a supervised diffusion loss such as denoising score-matching loss (DSM) or conditional flow-matching loss (CFM); e.g. the latter is used in FPO (https://t.co/umBUxE4KFq). However, the ELBO suffers from a KL gap: which causes the gradients of ELBO(a) and logp(a) to be misaligned:

ELBO(a) = log p(a) - D_KL(a)

*Idea*: Drive the KL gap down to zero after each policy gradient step, reminiscent of the EM algorithm for training GMMs. The E-step drives the KL gap to zero by computing the posterior distribution over latents *without changing the generative model.* Then, the M-step optimizes the ELBO; since the KL gap is already zero and can only increase, the M-step guarantees that the log-likelihood will also increase.

*Solution*: The rough equivalent of the "E-step" in diffusion models is self-distillation. You sample action labels from the current diffusion model and just do supervised learning (minimize ELBO) to match its own actions. This might seem useless because self-distillation does not modify the terminal action distribution at all! *The generative model and log-likelihoods log p(a) are unchanged*. But this drives the diffusion KL gap to zero! The KL gap in diffusion models is the distributional gap between the noising and denoising paths; a perfectly trained diffusion model that exactly reverses the noising process has zero KL gap. When this happens, D_KL(a) = 0. But zero is the global minimum for KL divergence, and so ∇D_KL(a) = 0. Which means that ∇ log p(a) = ∇ ELBO(a). The true and surrogate policy gradients are exactly aligned after self-distillation! Takeaway: Interleave policy update steps with self-distillation steps!

The smartest AI in finance is the one you never notice.

When people hear "AI," they usually think about chatbots or image generators.

But some of the most useful AI works quietly in the background of the financial apps millions of people use every day.

Here are a few simple examples:

➡️ Your banking app automatically groups your spending into categories like groceries, transport, and shopping, making it easier to understand where your money goes.

➡️ When you deposit a check with your phone, AI can help recognize the text and process the information more quickly.

➡️ If your card is suddenly used in a different city or country, AI may flag the activity and ask you to confirm it's really you before the payment is completed.

➡️ Customer support teams also use AI to handle routine questions, allowing human agents to spend more time helping with more complex issues.

Of course, AI isn't flawless.

It can misunderstand information or make incorrect predictions, which is why human review remains an important part of financial services.

For me, that's the most interesting part.

The goal isn't to replace people. It's to make everyday financial services faster, more efficient, and in some cases, more secure.

The more you understand how AI is being used, the easier it becomes to separate real innovation from marketing hype.

Keep learning, verify information through trusted sources, and always do your own research (DYOR).

🌸 Alpha - Fragments of Growth

A decade of travel, memories, and textures - now brought on-chain.

Created by fashion photographer and visual artist Jenya Vyguzov, the collection transforms textures captured across years of travel into handcrafted collage flowers , each petal representing a place, memory, or moment. No AI, no generative outputs, every piece is individually composed by hand.

What stands out here is the artist behind it. Jenya has spent over a decade working with brands like Vogue, GQ Style, Tatler, Elle, Puma, and IKEA, while building a distinctive visual language. His work was even selected for the official Paris Prints Textile Show poster, where he was given an entire floor for a solo exhibition.

⚠️ WL applications are coming soon. If you're interested in art NFTs, this is the stage where you want to be paying attention. Follow the artist and the project, turn on notifications, and be ready when WL applications open.
